articles.title: Who's running all those tiny RPKI servers?
articles.summary: Resource Public Key Infrastructure (RPKI) addresses are being published by multiple servers, including those run by cloud providers and independent companies, to help prevent routing incidents. These small servers operate on the global RPKI dataset anchored at five Regional Internet Registries and issue IP address space to networks. The existence of these smaller publication servers is a result of their need to contribute to the RPKI dataset while also operating independently.

articles.title: I tricked Claude into leaking your deepest, darkest secrets
articles.summary: Claude's memory system uses a naive two-part approach that distills conversations into a few paragraphs about the user and injects them into every single conversation. The second part, conversation_search, allows for retrieval of full conversation history on demand. However, this is only possible if an exfiltration vector can be found to get data out of Claude's sandbox, such as web browsing capabilities. If successful, it would allow malicious actors to detect and access the user's website without consent.

This vulnerability is fixed in Tailscale version 1.98.9 or newer, affecting nodes running Tailscale Serve or Tailscale Funnel on versions prior to that.

articles.summary: Measuring input latency on Linux: X11 vs Wayland VRR and DXVK, two popular technologies for improving system responsiveness in gaming applications, are being compared and contrasted in this article. The focus is on understanding how these technologies can be used to measure input latency and verify their effectiveness in reducing system lag. By using a device that simulates mouse clicks on a monitor and measures the time between the click and any changes detected on the screen, developers can gain insight into the performance of different Linux configurations and identify areas for optimization.

articles.title: git-absorb: git commit --fixup, but automatic
articles.summary: Git absorb automates the process of folding uncommitted changes into draft ancestor changesets in a working directory with draft changesets. This workflow enhancement allows developers to apply review feedback without having to manually make history modification rules or create opaque commits. By identifying which commits are safe to modify and which staged changes belong to each, git absorb automatically generates fixup commits for the problematic changes.

articles.title: Job queues are deceptively tricky
articles.summary: Job queues are deceptively tricky systems where submitting batch jobs, scheduling them, and running them is often managed using configuration files. This system arises naturally in situations requiring high throughput but low latency, such as continuous integration or data analysis summaries. Job queues can be problematic due to their tendency to become full or empty, requiring careful sizing and consideration of limits like tolerances and budgets for modular reasoning. Additionally, fault models are essential in describing assumptions related to errors and reliability in dependencies.
